coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of graphene quantum dots with temperature-sensitive properties from sea rice for rapid and highly selective detection of 4-nitrophenol
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Kun
Luo, Xiaoyu
Wu, Yulian
Liang, Zhibin
Kang, Xinhuang
Wen, Yanmei
coredata.dc:description: The green transformation of biomass materials into carbon nanomaterials with high application value has always been of great interest. In this study, we successfully synthesized temperature-sensitive and narrow-size distribution graphene quantum dots (GQDs) via mild oxidation, which were derived from 250 °C carbonized sea rice. This synthetic approach is green, supports large-scale operations, and consumes low amounts of energy. Through the use of TEM, DLS, AFM, XRD, Raman, FTIR, XPS, UV–vis, and PL technologies characterized GQDs. The GQDs exhibited good dispersion, temperature-sensitive properties, light stability, and tunable photoluminescence. The GQDs particle sizes were 1.20 ± 0.40 nm, and λ em = 458 nm at 340 nm excitation. Furthermore, we successfully detected trace amounts of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP), which exhibited a significant linear correlation between 0 and 1000 μM; the detection limit was 0.034 μM. The GQDs fluorescence probe was selective even with the distractors. Additionally, the quenching between the GDQs and 4-NP was caused by the internal filtering effect. Standard recovery experiments indicated that the recovery rates ranged from 97.6 % to 101.3 % for real water samples. Our research serves as a foundation for the creation of high-performance temperature-sensitive sensor devices for use in biomedical and environmental applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomass
Fluorescence
4-Nitrophenol detection
Green synthesis
Temperature-sensitive
Graphene quantum dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference ability of GQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of the GQDs. The inset is HRTEM image of the GQDs. (B) The GQDs particle size distribution histogram. (C) DLS particle size distribution diagram. (D) FFT analysis image of GQDs. (E) AFM image of GQDs, inset shows the thickness data of GQDs.
XRD patterns (A), Raman spectra (B), FT-IR spectra (C) of the GQDs and Cp. The C1s (D) and O1s (E) of the Cp and GQDs.

(A) Spectra for the UV–vis absorption (blue), fluorescence excitation (red), and fluorescence emission (black) of the GQDs. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs solutions at different λ em with 260–530 nm. (C) Normalized emission spectra of GQDs. (D) Three-dimensional fluorescence spectra of GQDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Changes in the fluorescent intensity of GQDs with pH (A), ion intensity (B), and irradiation of UV lamps (365 nm) over time (C).

(A) Effect of 4-NP (0–2000 μM) on the fluorescence intensity of GQDs. (B) Linear plot of F 0 − F vs. 4-NP concentration (0–1000 μM). (C) Effect of annealing time on fluorescence intensity. (D) Selectivity and anti-interference ability of GQDs for the detection of 4-NP. (E) UV–vis absorption spectra of 4-NP and PL spectra of GQDs.

Fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs from 30 to 80 °C (A). Linear GQDs fluorescence intensity changes during cooling (B) and heating (C). GQDs fluorescence intensity during cyclic temperature switching between 30 and 80 °C (D).
Synthesis of GQDs by mild oxidation.
Comparison of fluorescence methods detection of 4-NP.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of graphene quantum dots with temperature-sensitive properties from sea rice for rapid and highly selective detection of 4-nitrophenol

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemistry and Environment Science, Guangdong Ocean University, Zhanjiang 524088, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Chemistry and Environment Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Chemistry and Environment Science, Guangdong Ocean University, Zhanjiang 524088, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The green transformation of biomass materials into carbon nanomaterials with high application value has always been of great interest. In this study, we successfully synthesized temperature-sensitive and narrow-size distribution graphene quantum dots (GQDs) via mild oxidation, which were derived from 250 °C carbonized sea rice. This synthetic approach is green, supports large-scale operations, and consumes low amounts of energy. Through the use of TEM, DLS, AFM, XRD, Raman, FTIR, XPS, UV–vis, and PL technologies characterized GQDs. The GQDs exhibited good dispersion, temperature-sensitive properties, light stability, and tunable photoluminescence. The GQDs particle sizes were 1.20 ± 0.40 nm, and λ em = 458 nm at 340 nm excitation. Furthermore, we successfully detected trace amounts of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP), which exhibited a significant linear correlation between 0 and 1000 μM; the detection limit was 0.034 μM. The GQDs fluorescence probe was selective even with the distractors. Additionally, the quenching between the GDQs and 4-NP was caused by the internal filtering effect. Standard recovery experiments indicated that the recovery rates ranged from 97.6 % to 101.3 % for real water samples. Our research serves as a foundation for the creation of high-performance temperature-sensitive sensor devices for use in biomedical and environmental applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, carbon nanomaterial graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which are <10 nm in size, have gained significant attention for chemical sensor applications [1]. Although conventional semiconductor quantum dots or fluorophore molecules are commonly used in optical detection and imaging, they pose two major problems: metal ions from semiconductor quantum dots are highly toxic and the optical properties of fluorescent molecules are unstable [2]. GQDs exhibit considerable application potential in biological detection, biosensing, drug delivery, and biological imaging owing to their water solubility, adequate biocompatibility, stable optical properties, low toxicity, and photoluminescence characteristics [3–5]. In theory, the zero-bandgap in original graphene prevents it from glowing. However, when cut into nanoribbons, zero-band graphene exhibits incredible edge effects and quantum limitations. These physical properties also apply to GQDs, and become stronger as their size decreases [6,7]. However, the π-π conjugate system and surface functional groups of GQDs make them easy to functionalize, and it is simple for them to be doped and modified during synthesis [8]. They can be modified using various organic, inorganic, and biological materials as required, so they have great research, development and application potential [9].

4-Nitrophenol (4-NP) is frequently used in leather processing and the manufacturing of drugs, pesticides, explosives, and dyes [10,11]. 4-NP can exist in aquatic environments and soil for long periods of time because of its stability and solubility. The ingestion of 4-NP may induce a fever, headache, liver or kidney damage, and methemoglobinemia [12]. Moreover, trace levels of 4-NP can exert genotoxic and carcinogenic effects in organisms [11]. The maximum safe concentration of 4-NP in drinking water is 0.43 μM, as stipulated by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency [13]. Therefore, a highly selective and sensitive 4-NP assay is important for our health and the environment.

Sustainable development goals have led to the emergence of biomass materials as valid low-cost alternatives to traditional carbon-based materials. The synthesis of GQDs from biomass materials can reduce the cost of the preparation process and ensure the utilization of available resources. Studies have shown that, after high temperature carbonization, biomass materials can produce porous disordered carbon-based substances composed of nanoscale graphene, and that processing this disordered carbon at low temperatures could enable large-scale production of GQDs, bringing them one step closer to cheap commercial production [14–16]. However, accurately controlling the size of GQDs synthesized from biomass is often challenging, and the particle size distribution of most biomass-derived GQDs is 5–7 nm [16].

In this study, GQDs with narrow size distribution were synthesized from carbonized sea rice, using H2O2 as the sole oxidant. We developed a highly efficient method that is environmentally benign and readily scalable, unlike the traditional approaches for GQDs synthesis. Because of the internal filtration effect, 4-NP can quench the fluorescence of GQDs. Hence, the GQDs could quantitatively detect the presence of 4-NP. Owing to the temperature sensitivity of their fluorescence intensity, the prepared GQDs hold promise for applications in environmental detection, temperature-dependent fluorescence sensors, cancer diagnosis, and cancer treatment.

As seen in Scheme 1 , 2 g of milled sea rice was carbonized for 2 h in a muffle furnace by heating the sample to 250 °C at 8 °C/min. Approximately 0.1 g of the carbonized product (Cp) was added to a 50 mL KOH solution (0.1 M). After the dispersions were combined, 4 mL of H2O2 (30 %) was added to the solution, which was then stirred for 8 h at 26 °C. The final solution was yellow-brown in color. The pH was adjusted to 7.0 using H2SO4 (0.1 M), and the filtrate was obtained by filtration of the solution using a microporous membrane (0.22 μm). A purified solution of GQDs was produced via dialysis in a dialysis membrane at 1000 Da for 24 h.

4-NP detection: First, 100 μL of 4-NP at concentrations of 0, 100, 200, 300, 400, 500, 600, 700, 800, 900, 1000, 1200, 1400, 1600, 1800, and 2000 μM was placed in a solution of 2 mL H2O and 100 μL of the prepared GQDs. After the sample was shaken for 30 s at 26 °C and kept aside for 10 min, its PL spectra were measured by excitation at 340 nm.

Selectivity of 4-NP detection: 100 μL (100 mM) of ions, namely, K+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Al3+, Co2+, SO4 2−, PO4 3−, Br−, Cl−, and CO3 2−, and molecules, namely, alanine, glutamate, EDTA, ethanol, phenol, 4-tert-butylphenol, p-aminophenol, benzoic acid, 2,4-dinitrophenol, 2-NP, 3-NP, and 4-NP, were added to a solution of 2 mL H2O and 100 μL GQDs. Subsequent steps were the same as described for 4-NP detection.

Anti-interference of 4-NP detection: 100 μL (100 mM) of ions, namely, K+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Al3+, Co2+, SO4 2−, PO4 3−, Br−, Cl−, and CO3 2−, and molecules, namely, alanine, glutamate, EDTA, ethanol, phenol, 4-tert-butylphenol, p-aminophenol, benzoic acid, 2,4-dinitrophenol, 2-NP, and 3-NP were each mixed with 100 μL of 4-NP (100 mM), were added to a solution of 2 mL H2O and 100 μL GQDs. Subsequent steps were the same as described for 4-NP detection.

Tap water samples are collected at different local locations. Prior to analysis, all samples underwent filtering, using a 0.22 μm microporous membrane, after being centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min. Finally, a 20.0 mL sample was obtained. Next, the samples were fortified with 5–10 μM 4-NP and analyzed as described in Section 2.2 (100 μL sample + 2 mL H2O + 100 μL GQDs).

The Cp was obtained by performing carbonization in a muffle furnace using sea rice as the precursor. The SEM images (Fig. S1) show that Cp has a very rich and interesting carbon structure in a highly porous form. The GQDs were then synthesized by oxidizing the Cp. As shown in Fig. 1A, the TEM show the GQDs exhibited good dispersibility, average particle size of 1.20 ± 0.40 nm (Fig. 1B). The inset of Fig. 1A is HRTEM images of the GQDs have a single homogeneous lattice stripe, indicating that they exhibited good crystallinity. The inter-lattice of the GQDs was 0.34 nm, which was consistent with the (002) lattice of graphene [17].

Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements further prove the size and monodispersity of GQDs. The size of GQDs is distributed in 1–3 nm (Fig. 1C), which is consistent with TEM measurements. As shown in Fig. 1D, the Fast Fourier transform (FFT) pattern of the GQDs shows a regular hexagonal lattice, which is important evidence that the GQDs have a graphene crystal structure [18]. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) results revealed the transverse size and thickness of the GQDs (Fig. 1E). The thicknesses of the GQDs were in the 0.5–1.8 nm range, which indicates that the GQDs had a 1–5 layered graphene structure [15]. These data indicate that we have successfully produced GQDs.

XRD was used to evaluate the crystallinity of Cp and the GQDs. In Fig. 2A, both the Cp and GQDs have wide diffraction peaks, which are typical of amorphous carbon [19,20]. The GQDs have diffraction peaks at 2θ = 26.3°, 37.9°, 43.4°, attributed to the (002), (101), (100) crystal planes of graphene carbon [21,22]. The miscellaneous peaks in the XRD of the GQDs are K and S elements that have not been completely removed. Fig. 2B shows the Raman spectra of the Cp and GQDs with two representative bands D-band (1310–1340 cm−1) and G-band (1570–1580 cm−1), which are assigned to in-plane vibrations of the sp3 and sp2 carbon atoms, respectively. The relative intensities of the D-band and G-band are usually to characterize the extent of disorder or graphitization of carbon materials [23]. The ratio I D/I G of the GQDs (0.84) is close to that of the Cp (0.85), which indicates that the H2O2 oxidation has little effect on the structural defects of the carbon materials.

The chemical functional groups were analyzed by FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 2C). The absorption bands of the Cp at 1713, 2926, and 3201 cm−1 were coming from CO, NH, and OH stretching vibrations, respectively. Compared with those of the Cp starting material, the bands of 2900–3500 cm−1 in the spectrum of the GQDs exhibited a substantial increase. This observation indicates that the number of amino or hydroxyl groups may have increased on the surface of GQDs. These hydrophilic groups afford GQDs with good water solubility. The peak at approximately 1100 cm−1 of the Cp and GQDs were caused by COC/CO stretching vibrations. In addition, the absorption peak of GQDs at 1588 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibrations of the CC bonds of the benzene ring, and the absorption peak at 1387 cm−1 is attributed to the CCH bending vibration [24–29].

XPS was used for the elemental and structural analyses. The XPS peaks at 282, 396, and 528 eV were assigned to the C1s, N1s, and O1s orbitals, respectively (Fig. S2) [30,31]. The XPS profile of the Cp confirmed the C, N, and O with elemental contents of 84.19 %, 2.20 %, and 13.61 %, respectively. Similarly, the XPS profile of the GQDs confirmed the C, N, and O with elemental contents of 73.12 %, 2.45 %, and 24.43 %, respectively. Deconvolution of these profiles showed three peaks for C1s (Fig. 2D), these were attributed to three carbon bonding environments: the CC/CC peak was located at 285.2 eV, the CN/CO peak at 286.6 eV, and the CO/COC peak at 288.5 eV. The existence of the NH functional group was confirmed by the N1s peak at 400.2 eV (Fig. S3). Similarly, the three O1s peaks (Fig. 2E) at 531.2, 532.4, and 533.2 eV were attributed to the OH, CO, and CO bonds, respectively [30,31].

Fig. 3A shows the UV–vis and PL spectra of the GQDs. The blue line shows the UV–vis spectrum, with no distinct absorption peak. Under UV irradiation at 365 nm, the GQDs solution exhibits intense blue fluorescence (inset of Fig. 3A). In addition, GQDs exhibit characteristics that depend on the excitation wavelength of the light source. In Fig. 3B–C, the position of the emission peak and its intensity change when the excitation wavelength of the light is changed. The emission peak intensity initially increased and then decreased as the excitation wavelength increased from 260 nm to 530 nm. The fluorescence intensity peaked (λ em = 458 nm) when the excitation wavelength was 340 nm. At excitation wavelengths from 260 to 400 nm, the Stokes shifts are all >100 nm, indicating that excitation light interference can be effectively avoided during detection for GQDs. Fig. 3D shows the three-dimensional fluorescence spectrum of the GQDs, which reveals that they have a wide luminous range, and that they can be applied in the field of polychromatic spectroscopy. With quinine sulfate as the reference standard, the fluorescence quantum yield of GQDs is 0.81 %.

To evaluate the stability of the GQDs fluorescent probe, the influence of the pH of the solution, ionic strength, and irradiation of the UV lamps on the fluorescence intensity of the GQDs was examined. The fluorescence responses for the GQDs at different pH levels are shown in Fig. 4A. The oxygen-containing functional groups on the GQDs surface are protonated and deprotonated because of the pH variations. The GQDs exhibit their highest fluorescence intensities at pH = 7 [32]. Fig. 4B indicates that the ion strength influences the fluorescence intensity of the GQDs. With an increase in NaCl concentration to 4 M, the fluorescence intensity of the GQDs only decreased by 21 % indicating relatively good stability for the GQDs and demonstrating their potential for practical applications at relatively high ionic strengths. Moreover, we utilized a standard UV lamp with 220 V voltage and 6 W power, and irradiated GQDs with 365 nm light for 180 min (Fig. 4C). The negligible change in fluorescence intensity of GQDs demonstrated its good fluorescence stability.

The fluorescence intensity of the GQDs solutions with the addition of 4-NP between concentrations of 0 and 2000 μM was monitored at 340 nm to study the relationship between the GQDs quenching and 4-NP concentration. As shown in Fig. 5A, the suppression of the fluorescence intensity of the GQDs increases as the concentration of 4-NP increases. Fig. 5B shows the quenching efficiency F0-F of the GQDs, where F and F0 as the fluorescence intensities of the GQDs in the presence or absence of 4-NP, respectively. Based on the regression equation F0-F = 8.737[4-NP] + 609.23 (R2 = 0.996), the results show that F0-F is significantly linearly associated with the 4-NP concentration in the range of 0–1000 μM. The detection limit (LOD) was 0.034 μM (LOD = 3σ/S, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank specimen and S is the slope of the adjustment equation). This LOD is lower than most values reported previously (Table 1 ).

The change in fluorescence intensity with reaction time was measured, and the influence of response time on fluorescence intensity was studied (Fig. 5C). The results showed that the fluorescence intensity decreased sharply after 1 min between 4-NP and GQDs reaction. With prolongation of reaction time, fluorescence intensity has hardly changed, indicating that the fluorescence of GQDs could be completely quenched by 4-NP within 1 min. To ensure the completeness of the quenching reaction, 10 min was selected as the response time in this work. These results indicate that GQDs can be used as effective fluorescent sensors to determine trace amounts of 4-NP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference ability of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity and the anti-interference ability of GQDs are important for the application of GQDs in sensors. The selectivity of the prepared GQDs toward 4-NP was tested. Following a 10 min incubation period, the change in the fluorescence intensity was recorded when the GQDs were mixed with various amino acids, anions, cations, isomers, and other phenolic compounds. A light source with an excitation wavelength of 340 nm was used to determine the variation in the fluorescence intensity of the GQDs, and the results are shown in Fig. 5D.

We observed that, strong fluorescence quenching only occurred when 4-NP was added. However, when the other substances are added, GQDs exhibited a small change in fluorescence, indicating that GQDs have high selectivity for the detection of 4-NP. In addition, the anti-interference ability of the GQDs was examined in a mixture containing 4-NP and various interferents (Fig. 5D). The addition of the potential interferents did not affect the quenching of the GQDs by 4-NP, indicating that a sensing probe based on GQDs has good anti-interference ability, and that they can effectively and specifically detect 4-NP in mixtures containing other substances.

To study the mechanism of interaction between the GQDs and 4-NP, the UV spectrum of 4-NP and the PL spectra of the GQDs were analyzed. As seen in Fig. 5E, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of 4-NP overlaps with the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of GQDs, showing that the internal filtering effect leads to fluorescence quenching between 4-NP and GQDs [38].

To verify the practicality of GQDs as a detection probe for 4-NP, standard recovery experiments were performed on real samples. The results indicated that the recovery rates varied from 97.6 % to 101.3 %, and that the relative standard deviation (RSD) was <3.76 % (Table 2 ). This implied that this method could be used for the rapid detection of 4-NP in real samples with high reproducibility and accuracy.

Accurate measurement of temperature changes are key to understanding the reaction mechanisms of nano-systems and biochemical and physiological behaviors in cells [39]. The prepared GQDs exhibited significant temperature-dependent characteristics between 30 and 80 °C (Fig. 6A). The fluorescence intensity of the GQDs gradually decreased as the temperature was increased from 30 to 80 °C; when the temperature subsequently was decreased, the fluorescence intensity returned to its original value. Using the fluorescence intensity at 30 °C as a reference, a significant linear relationship was observed between the fluorescence intensity and temperature change. Correlation coefficients (R 2) of 0.991 and 0.999 were obtained for the heating and cooling processes, respectively (Fig. 6B–C).

In addition, the fluorescence intensity showed adequate reversibility when the temperature of the GQDs was alternated between 30 and 80 °C during six consecutive heating–cooling cycles (Fig. 6D). The sensitivity of the GQDs to the temperature change was due to the interactions between the surface functional groups containing oxygen and the hydrogen bonds [40]. Owing to this temperature-sensitive characteristic, GQDs are good candidates for application to the design of temperature sensors with high accuracy and reproducibility.

In this study, GQDs with good water solubility and tunable photoluminescence properties were synthesized on a large scale via mild oxidation using sea rice as a raw material. Using these GQDs, a simple, green, and fast 4-NP fluorescence-detection sensor was developed. The detection mechanism involved the internal filtering effect. The linear range of the method was 0–1000 μM 4-NP, and the LOD was 0.034 μM as the minimum value. This method was also used to detect 4-NP in actual samples. The recovery rates ranged from 97.6 % to 101.3 % and the RSD < 3.76 %, which indicated the significant repeatability and accuracy of the method. This study provides an effective new method for selectively and rapidly detecting 4-NP. GQDs exhibit adequate temperature-sensitive fluorescence properties in the temperature range of 30–80 °C, which is conducive to their application in thermally sensitive optical devices.

This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong [No. 2021A1515010304]; Project of Enhancing School with Innovation of Guangdong Ocean University [No. 230419054]; and Laboratory of Marine Chemistry Resource Utilization and Research [No. Q17092].
Conceptualization: Kun Luo, Xiaoyu Luo, Yulian Wu, Zhibin Liang; Writing Original Draft Preparation: Kun Luo; Writing Review & Editing: Yanmei Wen, Xinhuang Kang; Funding Acquisition: Yanmei Wen.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2023.109849.
